host-2020-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Twins and Religion' passage compares adolescents (12% genetics, 56% upbringing) with adults (44% genetics, 18% environment) — the relative weight of nature vs. nurture FLIPS as we age. That developmental shift is exactly what C names: different factors at different stages of life.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point here?' — so you need the single idea that the whole 'Twins and Religion' paragraph is built around, not a side number. With main-point items the trap is fixating on a vivid statistic; the headline is usually a comparison or shift the numbers serve.

steps.title: Locate the two number pairs
steps.text: Two pairs of figures structure the paragraph. Adolescents: 'genetics… accounted for only 12 percent of their religious identity, and a shared upbringing contributed 56 percent.' Adults: '44 percent of adults' religiosity could be attributed to genetics, and 18 percent had to do with their environment.' The numbers point in opposite directions across the two life stages.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Teenagers: how religious you are is mostly about how you were raised (56%), only a little about your genes (12%). Adults: it flips — your genes matter more (44%), your environment matters less (18%). So the SAME PERSON gets their religion from different sources at different ages.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: A few terms are worth pinning down. 'Religiosity' = how religious someone is. 'Disposition' = a built-in tendency to behave a certain way. 'Statistical model' = a math tool that estimates how much each factor (genes, upbringing) contributes to an outcome. 'Adolescence' = roughly the teenage years.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the paraphrase. A says genetic and environmental influences are 'equally important' — false; the proportions flip between life stages, so they're not held equal. B says personality is largely irrelevant for adults — also false; 44% from genetics (which the passage equates with personality dispositions) makes it the LARGEST factor. C says 'different factors determine people's views on religion during the course of their lives' — matches the flip directly. D says twins differ widely in religious beliefs over time — the passage talks about population-level proportions of variance, never about whether individual twins diverge.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The paragraph is built around a single contrast: the dominant cause of religiosity SHIFTS from environment (in adolescence) to genetics (in adulthood). That shift is the main point.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'genetic factors versus environmental influence' and assume the study found them roughly balanced overall — a tidy 50/50 takeaway feels like the natural moral of any nature-vs-nurture story.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 2's number pairs kill this — adolescence is 12/56 and adulthood is 44/18, never near equal. The whole point is that the balance MOVES, not that it settles at parity.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the word 'personality' and the adult figures, and read 18% environment as a sign that something non-genetic — like personality — has dropped out of the picture for grown-ups.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage equates genetics with 'dispositions for certain personality traits.' 44% adult genetics means personality is MORE important for adults, not irrelevant. Step 3 makes the directions explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a twin study is to expect divergence — twins drifting apart as they grow up is a familiar story shape.
distractors.why_wrong: The study reports POPULATION-LEVEL proportions of variance (how much genetics vs. environment explains across the sample), not whether individual twin pairs become more different. Step 5 catches the structural mismatch.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'main point' items with two number pairs, the headline is almost always the SHIFT between the pairs, not either pair on its own. Trigger to memorise: 'two sets of percentages for two different conditions → look for the option that names the contrast.'
host-2020-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Vivid percentages tempt you to pick an option that names a single figure ('genetics matters 44%' → 'genetics is important'). The headline lives in the COMPARISON between the figures, not in either one.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Rove Beetles' passage says the same parasitic disguise has evolved 'independently in at least 12… species' — a textbook case of convergent evolution that 'suggests… evolution may take similar and predictable paths.' That regular, predictable nature of evolution is exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied here?' — so you need the broader claim the passage is making, not just a restated fact. Implication questions reward the option that captures the author's TAKEAWAY, the lesson the example is meant to illustrate.

steps.title: Locate the headline claim
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. 'The deceit is so successful that it has evolved independently in at least 12 parasitic rove beetle species — a phenomenon called convergent evolution.' Then the explicit takeaway: 'it suggests that evolution may take similar and predictable paths whenever a certain scenario arises.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Twelve different beetle species, evolving separately, all ended up with the same trick: look like an ant, smell like an ant, eat the ants' young. When evolution produces the same answer twelve times over from different starting points, that's evidence evolution is FOLLOWING A RULE, not rolling dice. The headline: similar conditions → similar outcomes, predictably.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Convergent evolution' = unrelated species independently evolving the same trait because the same selection pressure pushes them there. 'Ploy' = a clever trick. 'Started from scratch' = re-rolled from the beginning. Knowing 'convergent evolution' nails the question by itself: it's literally the technical term for 'predictable evolution.'

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says beetle development is 'basically impossible to predict' — the OPPOSITE of the passage's takeaway; 12 independent convergences are predictability in action. B says ants and beetles will 'blend into one and the same species' — the passage describes mimicry, not interbreeding; the beetles eat the ants' young, hardly merging. C says the beetles 'violate most evolutionary principles' — also reversed; the passage says they CONFIRM a principle (convergence). D says the beetles' behaviour 'shows the regular nature of evolutionary development' — matches 'similar and predictable paths' directly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The whole paragraph is set up to illustrate one idea: convergent evolution shows that evolution, under similar pressures, repeats itself. 'Regular nature of evolutionary development' is a direct gloss of that.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'convergent evolution' as some weird oddity and conclude that beetle evolution is bizarre, surprising, impossible to predict.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's framing is the exact opposite — 12 independent convergences argue that evolution IS predictable under similar pressures. Step 2's closing quote ('similar and predictable paths') leaves no room for A.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'blend seamlessly into army ant societies' and read 'blend' as biological merging — two species becoming one.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Blend seamlessly' here means BEHAVIOURALLY pass as ants, not interbreed. The beetles 'devour the young' — predator and prey don't merge. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the distinction visible.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a story about parasites and deception is that something has gone WRONG in nature — a violation, a glitch in the system.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says the deceit CONFIRMS an evolutionary principle (convergence), not that it breaks one. Step 4 anchors this: 'convergent evolution' is a recognised pattern, not an anomaly.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-032.technique: When a biology passage names a technical term ('convergent evolution', 'natural selection', 'mimicry'), the right option usually re-states that term in plain words. Trigger to memorise: 'technical term in the passage → matching plain-English option.'
host-2020-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: A passage about deception and trickery (beetles disguising as ants) tempts you to pick options that sound 'weird' or 'surprising'. The author's framing here is the opposite — the deception is so widespread it's evidence of predictability.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The 'Time' passage opens by saying that humans 'like creatures ranging from amoebas to elephants, come with built-in equipment for perceiving some aspects of time' — built-in = genetic. The claim sweeps across all animals, so 'all animals are genetically programmed to sense the passage of time' (C) lands.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is claimed in the first paragraph?' Notice the scope-marker: FIRST paragraph. You're matching against ~6 lines of text, not the whole article. Cut your reading window to that single paragraph and the right option should fall out.

steps.title: Locate the first-paragraph claim
steps.text: The opening sentence carries the load: 'Humans, like creatures ranging from amoebas to elephants, come with built-in equipment for perceiving some aspects of time, such as the rhythms of night and day, and the turning of the seasons.' The phrase 'built-in equipment' = biologically wired in. The range 'amoebas to elephants' is shorthand for 'every animal.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Every animal — from the simplest single-celled creature up to the largest mammal — comes pre-installed with the ability to track time on a basic level (day/night, seasons). It's wired in, not learned. Humans share this basic equipment with the whole animal kingdom.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Two phrases matter. 'Built-in equipment' is a metaphor: born with it, biologically supplied — i.e. genetically. 'Amoebas to elephants' is a merism: the smallest and largest organisms used as bookends for 'all animals'. 'Perceiving' = sensing or noticing. So 'built-in equipment for perceiving time' = genetically supplied time-sensing capacity.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says humans and other animals share 'the same notion of time' — the FIRST paragraph says they share basic perception (day/night), but the next paragraphs explicitly contrast humans (who dice time into milliseconds and millennia) with other animals. So 'same notion' overreaches. B says some species are MORE dependent on time than others — the first paragraph doesn't rank species. C says all animals are 'genetically programmed to sense the passage of time' — direct match for 'built-in equipment for perceiving some aspects of time' across 'amoebas to elephants.' D says evolutionary pressure 'explains' time's significance — the paragraph mentions no evolutionary mechanism; it just states the capacity exists.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The first paragraph's load-bearing claim is universal animal capacity for basic time perception — and 'genetically programmed' is the natural plain-English gloss of 'built-in equipment'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'humans, like creatures ranging from amoebas to elephants' and conclude that humans and animals see time the same way — that's the surface comparison.
distractors.why_wrong: The next paragraph says exactly the opposite: 'What separates humans from other animals is that we dice it into units…' So humans share BASIC perception but not the same NOTION. Step 5 catches the scope mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers expect a hierarchy when a passage mentions species — bigger or smarter animals must be more dependent on something.
distractors.why_wrong: The first paragraph treats all animals symmetrically — amoebas and elephants are both bookends for 'all animals have built-in time perception.' No ranking appears. Step 2's bookend phrase makes this visible.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember biology class, 'built-in' often triggers 'evolutionary adaptation' as the natural cause — D feels like the textbook explanation.
distractors.why_wrong: The first paragraph never invokes evolutionary pressure as an EXPLANATION; it only states the capacity is there. D imports a mechanism the text doesn't supply. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps 'built-in' as a description, not a causal claim.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-033.technique: When a question scopes the answer to a specific paragraph ('according to the first paragraph', 'in the introduction'), match ONLY against that paragraph — even if later paragraphs say something more nuanced. The scope-marker is doing real work.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Built-in / biological language sounds like it implies an evolutionary mechanism, but the passage might only be describing a fact. Don't import causal explanations (evolution, adaptation) the text doesn't actually state.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The 'Time' passage argues that 'human time depends, in large part, on metaphor… in particular, on so-called conceptual metaphor, in which we think of something, in this case time, in terms of something else, in this case space.' Time is understood via space — that's exactly B: closely related to ideas about spatial dimensions.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks which statement about 'the human conception of time' is true according to the text. So you're looking for a claim the passage explicitly endorses about HOW HUMANS THINK ABOUT TIME — not what time IS, but what mental tools we use to grasp it.

steps.title: Locate the central claim
steps.text: Two sentences carry it. 'Human time depends, in large part, on metaphor… in particular, on so-called conceptual metaphor, in which we think of something, in this case time, in terms of something else, in this case space.' And: 'we build our understanding of duration, of time's passage and of sequences of events out of familiar spatial ideas such as size, movement and location.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: We don't think about time directly — we think about it through SPACE. 'A short weekend' uses size (a spatial idea). 'The week flew by' uses movement. 'The week ahead of us' uses location. Our basic mental tool for handling time is to imagine it as something that has size, position, and direction — i.e., spatial properties.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms anchor this question. 'Conceptual metaphor' = thinking of one thing (time) IN TERMS OF another (space) — not as decoration, as a cognitive tool. 'Spatial' = having to do with space, position, distance, size. 'Duration' = how long something lasts. So the claim is: our concept of duration borrows from our concept of physical size.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says our conception of time comes from an INABILITY to visualise time concretely — the passage doesn't argue from inability; it argues we DO have a concrete mental handle on time, just one borrowed from space. B says our conception is 'closely related to our ideas about spatial dimensions' — direct match for 'time… in terms of… space' and 'spatial ideas such as size, movement and location.' C says time and space are 'psychologically identical' — overreaches; the passage says time is UNDERSTOOD THROUGH space, not that the two are the same thing in our minds. D says all languages use the same expressions for time — the passage spends paragraphs on cross-linguistic VARIATION (English future-ahead, Aymara past-ahead, Yupno future-uphill), so D is directly contradicted.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The passage's structural claim is that human time-thinking is built on spatial thinking — and 'closely related to our ideas about spatial dimensions' is the cleanest English gloss of that.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'we build our understanding… out of familiar spatial ideas' as an admission that we CAN'T grasp time directly — so we resort to spatial substitutes.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames spatial metaphor as a powerful tool, not a workaround for inability. Step 3's examples (size, movement, location) are constructive, not compensatory. A imports a deficit the text doesn't claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'time is like space' and read it as 'time = space' in the mind — a tight equation rather than a metaphor.
distractors.why_wrong: Metaphor by definition means 'understanding A through B,' not 'A equals B.' Step 4's vocab gloss keeps the asymmetry visible: time gets its handles from space, but the two aren't psychologically identical.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim the opening and the closing sentences only, you might think the passage is celebrating a universal feature of language — and 'same expressions in all languages' fits that vibe.
distractors.why_wrong: The middle of the passage is built on counter-examples: English says 'week ahead', Aymara says 'a lot of time in front' to mean LONG AGO, Yupno puts the future uphill. The metaphors vary radically. Step 5 names the direct contradiction.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-034.technique: When a passage names a 'conceptual metaphor' or any cross-domain mapping (time → space, argument → war), the right option usually re-states the mapping in plain words. Trigger: 'X is understood through Y' in the text → option that says 'X is closely related to Y'.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: The passage explicitly contrasts a universal mental MECHANISM (space-for-time metaphor) with cross-cultural VARIATION in which specific metaphors get used. Don't conflate those two — options about universal expressions across languages confuse the mechanism with the surface form.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The passage introduces Whorf as 'famous for his idea that the language you speak guides the way you think' — i.e., linguistic patterns shape cognition. That's exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said about Benjamin Lee Whorf?' This is a direct-attribution question — you need a claim the passage MAKES about Whorf, not a guess about his broader legacy. Scan for sentences that name him.

steps.title: Locate the Whorf sentences
steps.text: Two sentences mention Whorf. The introducing one: 'Benjamin Lee Whorf, famous for his idea that the language you speak guides the way you think, keenly observed in the early 20th century that speakers of English and many other European languages talk of time as motion on a space.' The follow-up: 'Whorf also claimed that the Hopi, a Native American tribe, conceived of time in their language without spatial metaphors.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The passage tells us TWO things about Whorf. First, his famous big idea: language shapes thought — the way you speak influences the way you think. Second, his specific observations: English-speakers treat time as motion through space; he (mistakenly) thought the Hopi didn't.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Two terms matter. 'Linguistic patterns' = the structures of a language (its grammar, its metaphors, its habitual expressions). 'Cognition' / 'thought' = mental life. So 'linguistic patterns influence thought' = the language you use changes how you think — exactly the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis the passage paraphrases.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says Whorf was 'the first to focus on time from a human perspective' — the passage doesn't make any first-ever claim about Whorf; it just notes when he made his observations (early 20th century). B says he argued ALL languages use spatial metaphors for time — directly contradicted: Whorf claimed Hopi did NOT use spatial metaphors. C says he pioneered research into Native American languages — the passage uses his Hopi work as one example but never crowns him a pioneer of the field. D says he stressed the 'strong influence of linguistic patterns on thought' — direct match for 'the language you speak guides the way you think.'

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage's headline attribution to Whorf is his linguistic-relativity idea — that language guides thought — and D is the cleanest gloss of that.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'keenly observed in the early 20th century' as 'pioneered' — the early-20th-century framing tempts you to credit Whorf with being first.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Early 20th century' is a date, not a first-mover claim. The passage never says no one studied time-in-language before Whorf. Step 5 catches the leap from 'observed early' to 'first to focus'.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading gives 'Whorf… speakers of English and many other European languages talk of time as motion on a space' — sounds like he covered all languages.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next sentence contains his Hopi claim — that the Hopi DON'T use spatial metaphors for time. Whorf himself argued for the exception. Step 2's pair of sentences makes the contradiction explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: The Hopi reference attaches Whorf to Native American languages — 'pioneered' feels like a natural promotion of that link.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses Hopi as one illustration of Whorf's general thesis, not as the seed of a research field he created. C inflates a single example into a foundational role. Step 2's quotes don't support 'pioneer'.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On direct-attribution items ('what is said about X'), the correct option usually mirrors the passage's FAMED-FOR or INTRODUCED-BY phrasing — the bit that sets up who the person is. Skim for the appositive that introduces the figure; that's where the answer lives.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: A passage often gives both a general claim (language shapes thought) and a specific example (Hopi observation). Options will tempt you with the specific when the general is what's primarily attributed to the person.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The 'Time' passage explains that for the Aymara, the past is visualised in FRONT because they have SEEN it with their own eyes, while the future is behind because nobody has seen it. The split hinges on visual / personal experience — exactly A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the KEY FACTOR underlying the Aymara view of past and future. So you're looking for the single criterion the passage names — the one principle that decides which time goes in front, which goes behind. Watch out for options that name a RELATED but different criterion (reliability, distance, fact vs. fiction).

steps.title: Locate the criterion
steps.text: Two sentences carry the logic. 'For the Aymara, knowledge acquired through visual perception is taken to be certain and reliable.' And: 'For example, they can tell whether last year was dry or wet — they saw it with their own eyes. But how next year is going to be is anybody's guess — nobody has seen it. The known past is therefore conceived as being visually in front of them and the unknown future out of view behind them.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The Aymara organise time around what THEY have personally seen. If they witnessed it — last year's weather, anything in the past — it goes in front because their eyes confirm it. If they didn't — anything in the future, anything they only heard about — it sits behind, out of sight. Personal visual experience is the dividing line.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Two terms matter. 'Visual perception' = seeing with one's own eyes, first-hand sensory experience. 'Conceived' = mentally pictured or thought of. 'In front / behind' here are spatial metaphors for time. So 'visually in front' = personally seen and therefore mentally positioned forward; 'out of view behind' = not personally seen and therefore positioned back.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says the key factor is 'whether a particular event is personally experienced' — direct match for 'they saw it with their own eyes' as the line between front (past) and back (future). B says it's whether facts come from RELIABLE sources vs. unauthoritative ones — the passage frames reliability as a CONSEQUENCE of having seen it personally, not as the criterion itself; B blurs first-hand seeing into general source reliability. C says it's whether the event is distant past or near future — the passage's split is past-vs-future as a whole, not distant-vs-near. D says it's fact vs. fiction as judged by LISTENERS — the criterion is the SPEAKER'S having seen it, not what an audience believes.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The Aymara past-in-front / future-behind split tracks one variable: did the speaker personally witness the event? Everything else (reliability, certainty) follows from that.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'certain and reliable' and conclude the criterion is reliability of sources — and 'reliable vs. unauthoritative' sounds like a clean restatement of 'certain and reliable.'
distractors.why_wrong: Reliability is a DOWNSTREAM property in the passage — it follows because the Aymara saw it themselves. The criterion is PERSONAL VISUAL EXPERIENCE; reliability is a consequence. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the causal order visible.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'past' and 'future' and assume the question is about distance in time — distant past vs. near future feels like a natural axis.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's split is binary (past vs. future as wholes), not a sliding scale of distance. 'Last year' (recent) is treated the same as any other past event because the Aymara saw it. Step 5 names the structural mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a passage about Aymara grammar marking 'whether what is being said has been seen directly or learned from another source' is to read this as a credibility judgment by an audience.
distractors.why_wrong: The grammar marks the SPEAKER'S source, not the listener's verdict. D shifts the criterion from the speaker's witnessing to the audience's belief. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps 'visual perception' attached to the speaker.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'key factor underlying X' items, distinguish the CRITERION from its CONSEQUENCES. If the passage says 'A, therefore B', the question is asking for A, not B. Trigger: any option that names a downstream property (reliability, certainty, credibility) when the upstream cause (personal experience) is available — pick the upstream one.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: When a passage mentions both an evidential criterion (seeing it personally) and its emotional/epistemic consequence (it feels certain), distractors love to swap them. Stay anchored to the action — what does the speaker DO to qualify the event for front vs. back?

host-2020-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The 'Time' passage closes with the explicit claim that 'these different spatial metaphors reflect different cultural preoccupations, such as the mountainous landscape Yupno speakers call home' and that 'the particular metaphors we lean on… are a product of culture.' Culture shapes which time-metaphors a language uses — exactly C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what can be CONCLUDED about the relationship between time expressions and culture across languages. 'Concluded' is broader than 'stated' — you're looking for the takeaway the passage's structure builds toward, not just one sentence. Scan for the final-paragraph synthesis.

steps.title: Locate the synthesis
steps.text: Two sentences nail the link. 'These different spatial metaphors reflect different cultural preoccupations, such as the mountainous landscape Yupno speakers call home.' And the closing summary: 'The particular metaphors we lean on… are a product of culture and are much more malleable. Consequently, new spatial metaphors for our dearest abstract concepts will almost certainly enter the picture as our culture evolves.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The deep mechanism (time-as-space) is universal, but the SPECIFIC metaphors a language picks depend on the culture's environment and concerns. Yupno, in the mountains, makes the future uphill. Pormpuraaw, with strong cardinal-direction orientation, splits past and future east/west. And because cultures change, the metaphors will keep evolving too.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Cultural preoccupations' = what a culture cares about or organises its life around (mountains, cardinal directions, etc.). 'Malleable' = easy to shape or change. 'Product of culture' = generated by, caused by, dependent on culture. So the closing claim is: cultural context PRODUCES the time-expressions a language uses.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says NO clear connection between spatial metaphors and cultural contexts — the passage argues the OPPOSITE, with explicit examples (Yupno mountains, Pormpuraaw cardinal directions). B says the inventory of time expressions RESISTS cultural change — directly contradicted by 'are much more malleable… new spatial metaphors will almost certainly enter the picture as our culture evolves.' C says 'cultural features may account for the emergence of time expressions' — direct match for 'these different spatial metaphors reflect different cultural preoccupations' and 'product of culture.' D says the time-space link is unlikely to last — actually the passage says the link itself has 'deep evolutionary roots and is not likely to change'; only the SPECIFIC metaphors are malleable. D mixes up the layers.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The passage's two-layer structure is: a universal time-as-space mechanism PLUS culture-specific metaphors. The question is about the second layer, and C names the causal direction (culture → metaphor) correctly.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'all cultures make use of time-as-space metaphors' as 'the metaphors are universal and culture doesn't really matter.'
distractors.why_wrong: The next sentences immediately do the opposite: Yupno mountains → uphill future; Pormpuraaw → east/west. The mechanism is universal; the specific metaphors are culture-specific. Step 5 names the directly contradicting evidence.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'deep evolutionary roots' and conclude that the whole vocabulary of time is stable and resistant to change.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next clause says 'particular metaphors… are a product of culture and are much more malleable… new spatial metaphors will almost certainly enter the picture as our culture evolves.' The roots are deep; the surface forms are not. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps 'malleable' visible.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'evolutionary' from the closing paragraph, D's 'unlikely to be of lasting duration' for the time-space link might feel like it fits a story about change.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says the LINK (time-as-space metaphor as a mental mechanism) is durable; only the SPECIFIC metaphors change. D inverts the layers. Step 5 catches the mix-up.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-037.technique: When a passage explicitly distinguishes between a universal MECHANISM and culture-specific FORMS, questions about 'the relationship between X and culture' almost always target the forms (the surface, the malleable part). Trigger: 'universal A, but X varies by culture' → the answer concerns X.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Two-layer passages (universal layer + variable layer) generate distractors that swap the two layers. Always identify which layer the question is asking about before matching options.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Meat' passage notes that U.S. per-capita beef consumption peaked in 1976 and has fallen 40%, that chicken outpaced beef in 2010, and that Argentina's beef appetite has fallen — three Western data points all pointing down. That decline relative to a few decades ago is exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied here?' — an implication question on a short paragraph. You need the claim that pulls the data points together, not any single statistic. Watch for global vs. regional scope and for positive vs. negative trend language.

steps.title: Locate the trend data
steps.text: Three data points sit in the passage. 'Chicken outpaced beef in the United States in 2010.' 'Per capita beef consumption in the U.S. peaked in 1976 at 91.5 pounds a year; it has since fallen more than 40 percent.' 'Argentina's famous appetite for beef has fallen because of cholesterol consciousness and economic downturns.' Counter-note: 'In countries where meat is a newly affordable option, animal protein is a boon, not a debate.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: In the West — the U.S. and Argentina specifically — beef-eating is way down compared to forty years ago. Chicken has overtaken beef in the U.S. Health worries and economic pressure are cutting into beef consumption. But in places that have just gotten richer enough to afford meat, eating it is celebrated, not contested. So the controversy and decline are a WESTERN story.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Polarizing' = creating sharp disagreement. 'Per capita' = per person — a way of comparing across populations of different sizes. 'Cholesterol consciousness' = awareness of cholesterol as a health concern, leading to reduced fatty-meat consumption. 'Outpaced' = surpassed, overtook.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says eating meat is becoming 'increasingly controversial across the globe' — the passage explicitly limits the controversy to the West and says newly prosperous countries treat meat as 'a boon, not a debate.' Global scope overreaches. B says chicken has been more popular than beef since the LATE 20TH CENTURY — the passage gives one date, 2010, which is the early 21st century, not the late 20th. The date is wrong. C says the world market price has reached an all-time low — the passage talks about consumption, not price; no price data appears. D says 'Attitudes to meat in parts of the Western world are less positive today than a few decades ago' — matches the U.S. (down 40% from 1976) and Argentina (cholesterol consciousness, downturns) decline directly, with the correct regional scope ('parts of the Western world').

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The passage frames a clear Western decline (U.S., Argentina) against a non-Western rise. D captures the Western half of that contrast and respects the passage's regional scope.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'meat… can be polarizing' and 'controversy' as a global headline — the opener sounds like the takeaway.
distractors.why_wrong: The closing sentence explicitly carves out 'countries where meat is a newly affordable option' as places where 'animal protein is a boon, not a debate.' The controversy is not global. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the regional split visible.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Chicken outpaced beef in the United States in 2010' and conclude this trend has been running for decades — the late-20th-century framing feels close enough.
distractors.why_wrong: 2010 is the EARLY 21st century, not the late 20th. The passage gives no claim about chicken's popularity before 2010. B fabricates a longer timeline. Step 5 names the date mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the U.S. consumption peaking and falling, you might infer that falling demand pushed prices to historic lows — a natural economic chain of thought.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage talks about CONSUMPTION (pounds eaten per person), not PRICE. Demand and price aren't equivalent; world beef prices in fact rose during the 2010s. C imports market data the passage never supplies. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps 'per capita' as a consumption measure.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On implication items with regional data, identify the SCOPE of each data point (U.S., Argentina, 'newly affordable' countries) before matching. The right option's scope will match the passage's scope; wrong options usually overreach to 'globe' or underreach to one country.
host-2020-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: An opening sentence ('meat can be polarizing') often sets up a contrast that the rest of the paragraph develops. Don't read the opener as the conclusion — the paragraph's middle and ending refine and qualify it.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'Dalits' passage tells us the Memorial was COMMISSIONED by a Dalit former chief minister, and that 'caste-based discrimination is illegal under the Indian Constitution.' A Dalit memorial and a constitutional ban are both deliberate state-level moves to address historical inequality — exactly A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied here?' — so you're looking for the claim the passage gestures at without stating outright. Implication questions reward the option that the FACTS in the passage MAKE PLAUSIBLE, not the option that paraphrases a single line.

steps.title: Locate the institutional moves
steps.text: Two sentences carry the institutional facts. 'The National Dalit Memorial and Green Garden, which opened recently, stands out. It is dedicated to the historically oppressed Dalit community and was commissioned by one of the few Dalit women to rise to power in India: the former chief minister of the state of Uttar Pradesh, Mayawati.' And the legal note: 'Caste-based discrimination is illegal under the Indian Constitution.' Plus the qualifier: 'the social and legal reality is far more complex.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: India has taken concrete steps to confront the historical mistreatment of Dalits: it built a national memorial dedicated to them, it elevated a Dalit woman to a top political office (chief minister), and it banned caste discrimination in its constitution. These are official, state-level acts. The passage adds that everyday reality is messier — but the STEPS themselves are real.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Commissioned' = officially ordered to be built. 'Marginalized' = pushed to the edges of society. 'Constitution' = the foundational law of the country. 'Come to grips with' (in option A) = to start dealing seriously with something. So the question is whether the passage shows India trying to address Dalit inequality at all — and the memorial + the constitutional ban both say yes.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says India 'has taken measures to come to grips with social inequality' — matches the constitutional ban on caste discrimination AND the national memorial commissioned by a Dalit chief minister. Both are measures, both are official, both target Dalit inequality. B says Dalits have seen great changes in their EVERYDAY treatment — the passage explicitly says 'the social and legal reality is far more complex,' which signals that everyday treatment HASN'T changed simply. C says India is 'officially governed by its historically rooted caste system' — directly contradicted: caste discrimination is illegal under the Constitution, so the OFFICIAL system rejects caste. D says Dalits are a 'socially close-knit group… who tend to keep to themselves' — the passage describes Dalits as 'shunned, marginalized' from the outside, not as choosing isolation.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The passage stacks two concrete state-level measures — a national memorial and a constitutional ban on caste discrimination — that together imply official efforts to address Dalit inequality.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'the National Dalit Memorial' and 'a Dalit woman… rise to power' as evidence that things have changed dramatically for ordinary Dalits day-to-day.
distractors.why_wrong: The closing sentence explicitly contradicts B: 'the social and legal reality is far more complex.' Institutional changes ≠ everyday changes. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the layer separation visible.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the historical caste system and 'historically rooted,' you might infer that the system is still officially in place — 'historically rooted' sounds like 'still rooted.'
distractors.why_wrong: The constitutional ban is explicit: 'caste-based discrimination is illegal under the Indian Constitution.' The OFFICIAL government has formally rejected caste rule, even if SOCIAL reality is messy. Step 5 names the contradiction.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'a wide cluster of social groups' is to read it as a tight, self-organising community.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames Dalits as being TREATED a certain way by others (shunned, marginalized, excluded), not as choosing to keep to themselves. D mistakes external exclusion for internal cohesion. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps 'marginalized' attached to outside forces.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On implication items where the passage stacks INSTITUTIONAL facts (a law, a memorial, a high-profile appointment), the right option usually names the overarching policy direction those facts add up to. Trigger: 'constitutional ban + commissioned monument + rise to power' → 'official measures to address X.'
host-2020-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: When a passage explicitly notes that 'the social and legal reality is far more complex,' that qualifier is a SHIELD against options that claim everyday or grass-roots change. The institutional layer can move while the daily layer lags.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Awe' passage describes experiments where participants are exposed to awe-inducing scenes (breathtaking natural videos, towering eucalyptus trees) and afterwards behave more 'prosocially' — more helpful, more ethical, more generous. Grand scenery causes some people to act more generously — exactly B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said here?' — a direct-claim question on a short paragraph. You need an option the passage either states or clearly demonstrates. The trap on awe-and-generosity studies is sliding between the trigger (awe) and the outcome (prosocial behaviour).

steps.title: Locate the key claim
steps.text: The setup: 'People who report feeling awe more often are, in fact, more generous… those who frequently felt awe gave away more tickets.' Then the experimental causal claim: 'They evoked awe through videos of breathtaking natural scenes and by taking subjects outside to gaze upward at towering eucalyptus trees. In every case, those who experienced awe behaved in what psychologists call a more prosocial way, being more helpful or making more ethical decisions.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Researchers triggered awe in people by showing them sweeping natural scenery (videos and real trees). After feeling awe, people gave more, helped more, behaved more ethically. The CAUSE is exposure to grand, breathtaking scenes; the EFFECT is more generous behaviour. The study claims an experimentally established link from scenery to giving.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Awe' = the feeling of wonder and smallness in the face of something grand. 'Prosocial' = behaviour that benefits others (helping, sharing, donating). 'Grandiose scenery' (in option B) = breathtaking, vast natural views — the same kind of stimulus the experiments used. 'Evoked' = brought out, triggered.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says HAPPY people are more generous than UNHAPPY people — the passage isn't about happiness; it's about awe specifically, and awe is a distinct emotion from happiness. B says 'exposure to grandiose scenery may lead some people to behave more generously' — direct match for 'breathtaking natural scenes' + 'towering eucalyptus trees' as triggers, with 'more helpful' / 'prosocial' as outcome. The hedge 'may lead some' matches an experimental finding well. C says generous people are more 'outgoing' and socially active — the passage doesn't characterise generous people as extraverts; it links generosity to awe-experience frequency, which is internal, not social. D says awe, pride, and amusement are associated with SIMILAR behaviour — the passage uses pride and amusement as CONTRAST conditions ('induced awe in some… and other emotions such as pride or amusement in others'), and only awe produced the prosocial effect.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The whole paragraph is built to support one causal claim: grand scenery → awe → more generous behaviour. B names that chain in plain English with appropriate hedging.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'feeling awe more often are, in fact, more generous' as 'feeling good → more generous,' collapsing awe into general happiness.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage carefully separates awe from other positive emotions like pride and amusement, and only awe drives the prosocial behaviour. Step 5 names the contrast experiments that rule out general happiness as the driver.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'more generous' and 'prosocial' and conclude generous people must be extraverted social butterflies — the words feel adjacent.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage links generosity to a specific INNER STATE (awe), not to a personality trait like extraversion. 'Outgoing' is a profile the text never builds. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the chain as 'awe → generosity', not 'extraversion → generosity'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the three emotions named — awe, pride, amusement — you might assume the passage treats them as similar feelings with similar effects.
distractors.why_wrong: Pride and amusement are explicitly the CONTROL conditions used to isolate awe's effect — they were tested specifically because they DIFFER from awe. The whole experimental design hinges on awe being different. Step 5 catches the inverted reading.

host-2020-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'what is said' items about experimental findings, trace the CAUSE-EFFECT chain the paragraph builds: stimulus → emotion → behaviour. The right option names the same chain at the right granularity. Trigger: 'experiment evoked X, participants then did Y' → option that says 'X may lead to Y.'
host-2020-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: When a study uses contrast conditions (awe vs. pride vs. amusement), distractors often treat the contrasts as equivalents. Read 'we tested A AND B AND C' as 'we are distinguishing A from B and C', not 'A, B, C all act the same.'

host-2020-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The gap sits inside a 'However' contrast: a couple of drinks REDUCE stroke risk, BUT any more 'enhances the risk of the most deadly strokes.' The 'however' demands a verb meaning 'increases' — only 'enhances' does that here, so the answer is A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a single-word cloze: pick the verb that completes 'any more than that ___ the risk of the most deadly strokes.' All four options (enhances, modifies, decreases, improves) are verbs that can take 'the risk' as an object, so semantics — not grammar — decides the answer. Cloze rule one: read what comes BEFORE and AFTER the gap and let the connective logic pick the word.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Sentence in full: 'A couple of drinks a day reduce the risk of the most common type of stroke by about a tenth. However, any more than that ___ the risk of the most deadly strokes.' Two halves: 'a couple = reduces risk', then 'However, more than that = does the opposite to risk.' The contrast word 'however' forces the gap to point in the OPPOSITE direction from 'reduce.'

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Pattern: 'A reduces X. However, B ___ X.' For the contrast to work, the gap verb must mean 'increases.' So the question reduces to: which of these four words means 'increases the risk of stroke'? The other three meanings (modifies, decreases, improves) don't move risk UPWARD.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Four words to sort. 'Enhances' usually means 'makes better' in everyday English, BUT it also means 'increases / intensifies' when paired with neutral or negative nouns ('enhanced risk', 'enhanced danger' both mean greater). 'Modifies' = changes (direction unspecified) — too neutral for a 'however' contrast. 'Decreases' = reduces — same direction as the first sentence, breaks the contrast. 'Improves' = makes better — would mean LOWER risk, also breaks the contrast.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option in the slot. 'However, any more than that ENHANCES the risk' — the contrast works; 'enhances' = increases when paired with risk. 'However, any more than that MODIFIES the risk' — too vague, no contrast. 'However, any more than that DECREASES the risk' — same direction as 'reduce', no contrast at all. 'However, any more than that IMPROVES the risk' — improving risk would mean lowering it, again no contrast. Only A produces the contrast 'however' demands.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The 'However' between the two halves of the sentence locks the gap into a verb meaning 'increases the risk' — 'enhances' is the only option that delivers that direction when paired with 'risk.'

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'modifies' as a safe, generic verb that fits anywhere — change is always plausible.
distractors.why_wrong: 'However' demands a SPECIFIC direction, not just any change. 'Modifies' doesn't say whether risk goes up or down, so it can't carry the contrast. Step 3's contrast logic rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the first sentence ('reduce the risk… by about a tenth') and slot the same verb idea into the second slot — repetition feels natural.
distractors.why_wrong: The second sentence opens with 'However' — a contrast word that forces the OPPOSITE direction. 'Decreases' would just repeat 'reduces' and make 'however' nonsensical. Step 5 plugs and breaks the sentence.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'improves' as a positive verb, you might read 'improves the risk' as 'makes the risk smaller' — a polite way of saying 'reduces.'
distractors.why_wrong: Even if 'improves the risk' could mean lower risk, that's the SAME direction as 'reduce' in the first sentence, breaking the 'however' contrast. Step 5 catches the direction error.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-031.technique: Cloze with a contrast word ('however', 'but', 'in contrast'): the gap verb almost always points in the OPPOSITE direction from the previous clause. Skim the sentence for 'reduce / however / ___' patterns and lock the direction before choosing vocabulary.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: 'Enhances' looks like a positive word, so students rule it out when the noun is something bad ('the risk'). Remember: 'enhance' is also a direction-neutral intensifier — 'enhanced risk' just means 'greater risk.'

host-2020-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The gap sits in 'The study adds to the ___ around the health benefits of light drinking.' The paragraph above sets up competing claims (alcohol may help blood clots; but blood pressure may offset benefit) — that's a debate, not a fact or measurement. The right word is 'controversy', so the answer is C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a noun cloze: pick the noun that completes 'adds to the ___ around the health benefits of light drinking.' All four options (ignorance, prejudice, controversy, impact) are abstract nouns that could grammatically follow 'adds to the.' Again semantics decides. Read what surrounds the gap and let the rhetorical situation pick the noun.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The two sentences just before the gap: 'Researchers believe that alcohol could hamper the formation of dangerous blood clots. But they caution against drinking to protect the brain, saying that a rise in blood pressure could offset any benefit.' Then: 'The study adds to the ___ around the health benefits of light drinking.' A claim is made, then a counter-claim is added — that's a debate in motion.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Pattern: 'Some scientists say X. But others caution Y. The study adds to the ___ around Z.' For 'adds to' to work, the noun must be something a study CAN add to by contributing new claims — a disagreement, a discussion. The two-sided framing in steps 2 demands a word that names a back-and-forth, not a one-sided concept.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Four nouns to sort. 'Ignorance' = lack of knowledge — a study would REDUCE ignorance, not add to it. 'Prejudice' = unfair preconception — studies don't usually 'add to prejudice,' and the framing isn't about bias. 'Controversy' = public disagreement, a debate with two or more sides — fits 'add to' (each new study fuels the debate) and matches the 'some say… but others caution' setup. 'Impact' = effect or influence — you can 'add to the impact' of something, but the surrounding text isn't about effects, it's about a debate over health benefits.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. 'Adds to the IGNORANCE around the health benefits' — backwards; new studies clarify, they don't darken. 'Adds to the PREJUDICE around the health benefits' — 'prejudice around health benefits' doesn't fit standard English; prejudice is against people, not against research findings. 'Adds to the CONTROVERSY around the health benefits' — clean fit: a new study with a mixed result feeds the existing debate. 'Adds to the IMPACT around the health benefits' — odd collocation; 'impact' is usually OF something, not 'around' it.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The setup is explicitly a two-sided debate (helps clots vs. raises blood pressure), and 'controversy' is the noun that captures a multi-sided argument a new study can 'add to.'

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'much more to understand' (which appears later in the passage) as a clue that the field is full of ignorance.
distractors.why_wrong: A study CONTRIBUTES knowledge, so it would REDUCE ignorance, not add to it. The collocation 'adds to the ignorance' is backwards. Step 5 plugs and fails.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'controversial' and 'prejudiced' as adjacent disagreement-words, 'prejudice' might feel close to 'controversy.'
distractors.why_wrong: 'Prejudice' is unfair bias against a group, not a debate over evidence. 'Prejudice around the health benefits of light drinking' isn't standard English. Step 4's vocab gloss keeps the distinction sharp.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'study' + 'health benefits' is to talk about the study's impact or effect.
distractors.why_wrong: The fixed preposition is 'add to the impact OF', not 'add to the impact AROUND.' The gap uses 'around', which collocates with debates and discussions (controversy, conversation, discourse), not effects. Step 5 catches the collocation mismatch.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-032.technique: Cloze with 'add to the ___ around X': the noun is almost always a debate-word (controversy, discussion, debate). The preposition 'around' signals an ongoing conversation, not an effect or a state. Trigger: 'add to the ___ around' → pick controversy / debate / discussion.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Students sometimes lean on a single sentence to pick a cloze answer. Here the paragraph BEFORE the gap (two competing claims) is what locks in 'controversy'. Always read the sentence in its rhetorical context, not in isolation.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: The gap completes 'resulting from a blockage in the blood ___ to the brain.' What can a blockage block? The flow of blood — and the standard English collocation is 'blood supply'. The answer is D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a noun cloze inside a compound noun phrase: 'blockage in the blood ___ to the brain.' You're picking the second half of a 'blood ___' compound. All four options (track, pressure, drain, supply) can pair with 'blood' grammatically, so the question is which compound makes physiological sense in 'a blockage in the blood ___ to the brain.'

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Sentence in full: 'A recent investigation… suggests a benefit in protecting against ischemic strokes, which make up the bulk of cases, resulting from a blockage in the blood ___ to the brain.' Definition embedded: ischemic stroke = caused by a BLOCKAGE in something that runs TO the brain. So you need the noun that names what blood does when it travels TO an organ.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Pattern: 'a blockage in the blood ___ to the brain.' For 'blockage' to make sense, the noun must name a FLOW or a PATHWAY — something a clot can stop. The preposition 'to' marks direction (blood travelling toward the brain), so the noun should describe the delivery of blood to a target organ.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Four compounds to test. 'Blood track' = not a standard English collocation; 'track' suggests animal trails or sports, not circulation. 'Blood pressure' = the force blood exerts on vessel walls — a number, not a pathway; you can't have 'a blockage in the blood pressure to the brain' (pressure isn't a route). 'Blood drain' = backwards; a 'drain' carries fluid AWAY, but the sentence uses 'to the brain.' 'Blood supply' = the standard medical phrase for the delivery of blood to an organ — and a blockage in the blood supply is exactly what causes an ischemic stroke.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. 'A blockage in the blood TRACK to the brain' — non-idiomatic; we don't talk about blood tracks. 'A blockage in the blood PRESSURE to the brain' — categorical mismatch; pressure isn't something you block. 'A blockage in the blood DRAIN to the brain' — direction-wrong; drains carry fluid away, not 'to the brain.' 'A blockage in the blood SUPPLY to the brain' — standard medical idiom; matches both 'blockage' (you block a supply) and 'to the brain' (delivery direction).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Blood supply' is the established medical collocation for blood being delivered to an organ, and a blockage in that supply is the textbook definition of an ischemic stroke — which the sentence is literally defining.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'blockage' is to imagine a physical path — and 'track' sounds like a path you can block.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Blood track' isn't English; the natural pathway word is 'vessel' or 'supply.' Step 4's vocab gloss flags 'track' as the wrong domain (sports, animals).

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers know 'blood pressure' as the dominant 'blood ___' collocation in health writing and reflexively pick it.
distractors.why_wrong: Blood pressure is a NUMBER (force per unit area), not a pathway. You can't have 'a blockage in a number to the brain.' The grammar fits but the physiology doesn't. Step 5 plugs and breaks.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember that strokes involve blood leaving where it shouldn't, 'drain' might feel related — drainage and bleeding live in the same medical neighbourhood.
distractors.why_wrong: A 'drain' carries fluid AWAY from a body part, but the sentence specifies 'to the brain' (delivery, not removal). Direction is reversed. Step 4's vocab gloss makes the direction explicit.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-033.technique: Cloze for medical terms: the right answer is usually the standard COLLOCATION ('blood supply', 'heart rate', 'lung capacity'), not a grammatically possible alternative. Train your ear for which 'blood + noun' pairings actually appear in health writing.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: When a sentence DEFINES a term ('ischemic strokes… resulting from a blockage in the blood ___ to the brain'), the gap is part of a textbook definition. Pick the word that COMPLETES THE STANDARD DEFINITION — here, 'blood supply' is part of how every textbook describes ischemic stroke.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: The gap is in 'the ___ effect of alcohol consumption on blood pressure — a major risk factor for stroke — may increase the risk of another type of stroke.' Alcohol's effect on blood pressure is a NEGATIVE one (raises pressure, raises stroke risk). The adjective that names a harmful effect is 'adverse', so the answer is C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is an adjective cloze: pick the adjective that completes '___ effect of alcohol consumption on blood pressure.' Four options (beneficial, neutral, adverse, modest) are adjectives that can modify 'effect,' so semantics decides. Read the full sentence: the gap-adjective describes how alcohol affects blood pressure, and the result MAY 'increase the risk of another type of stroke.'

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The Larsson quote in full: 'Previous research has found an association between alcohol consumption and lower levels of fibrinogen… While this may explain the connection between light to moderate alcohol consumption and lower ischemic stroke risk, the ___ effect of alcohol consumption on blood pressure — a major risk factor for stroke — may increase the risk of another type of stroke.'

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Pattern: 'While X helps with ischemic strokes, the ___ effect on blood pressure may INCREASE the risk of another type of stroke.' The 'while' sets up a contrast: alcohol's good side (lower clot risk) vs. its bad side (raising blood pressure, raising another stroke risk). The gap-adjective must name the BAD side — something negative or harmful.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Four adjectives to sort. 'Beneficial' = helpful, good — wrong direction; the effect raises stroke risk, not lowers it. 'Neutral' = neither helpful nor harmful — wrong; the effect actively increases risk. 'Adverse' = harmful, unfavourable, working against you — direct match for an effect that 'may increase the risk of another type of stroke.' 'Modest' = small in size — direction-neutral; talks about MAGNITUDE, not whether the effect is good or bad, so it doesn't carry the 'while X, but Y' contrast.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. 'The BENEFICIAL effect on blood pressure may increase the risk of stroke' — internal contradiction; beneficial effects don't increase risks. 'The NEUTRAL effect on blood pressure may increase stroke risk' — also contradictory; neutral means no effect, so it can't change risk. 'The ADVERSE effect on blood pressure may increase stroke risk' — coherent; an adverse effect is exactly the kind that raises risk. 'The MODEST effect on blood pressure may increase stroke risk' — grammatically fine but loses the contrast 'while' demands; we need a direction (good vs. bad), not a size word.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'While X… the ___ effect… may INCREASE risk' structure forces a negative adjective, and 'adverse' is the only option that names a harmful effect directly.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'effect of alcohol' positively, since the paragraph started by saying alcohol lowers some stroke risks — 'beneficial' continues that frame.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'while' contrast flips the frame: the SAME paragraph admits alcohol's downside on blood pressure, which RAISES other stroke risks. A beneficial effect can't raise risk; the words are mutually contradictory. Step 5 plugs and breaks.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the study is cautious and balanced, 'neutral' might sound like a measured, scientific word.
distractors.why_wrong: A neutral effect by definition doesn't change anything, so it can't 'increase the risk of another type of stroke.' The sentence demands a directional effect. Step 4's vocab gloss anchors the categorical mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'a major risk factor' and read the effect as a 'modest' nudge upward — the paragraph's careful tone fits 'modest.'
distractors.why_wrong: 'Modest' is about SIZE, not DIRECTION. The 'while X helps, but the ___ effect HURTS' contrast needs a word that names harm, not a word that measures it. Step 3's contrast logic excludes magnitude-only adjectives.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-034.technique: Cloze with 'while X is good, the ___ effect of Y increases harm': the gap demands an adjective that POLARIZES the contrast — beneficial vs. adverse, helpful vs. harmful. Trigger: any sentence with 'while' + a positive clause needs a negative adjective on the other side.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Magnitude adjectives ('modest', 'mild', 'considerable') feel safe and scientific, but they don't carry direction. When the sentence's structure demands good-vs-bad, pick a directional adjective, not a sizing one.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The gap is in 'No ___ has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke.' What can researchers fail to FIND BETWEEN two things? A connection — and the standard scientific collocation is 'link between A and B'. So the answer is B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a noun cloze with a fixed preposition pair: 'No ___ has been found BETWEEN A AND B.' Whatever fills the gap, it has to be a noun that collocates with 'between' to describe a relation. All four options (evidence, link, resemblance, trace) are nouns that COULD appear with 'between', but only one is the standard scientific collocation for 'a relation between A and B that researchers look for.'

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Sentence in full: 'About 15 per cent of strokes are the result of blood vessels bursting within the brain. This type, known as haemorrhagic, tends to be fatal. No ___ has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke. However, people who had more than four drinks a day were 67–82 per cent more likely to suffer one.' The whole study question has been 'does drinking relate to this stroke type?' — and the answer here is 'no, not for LIGHT drinking.'

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Pattern: 'No ___ between X and Y.' In medical research, what researchers test is whether two things are CONNECTED. They look for a LINK (correlation, association) and report whether they found one. The natural negation is 'no link found.' The gap-noun should name 'a connection between two variables.'

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Four nouns to sort. 'Evidence' = proof — you'd say 'no evidence FOR' something or 'no evidence OF' a connection, not 'no evidence BETWEEN.' Collocation is wrong. 'Link' = connection, association — the textbook scientific term, used exactly in 'a link between A and B.' 'Resemblance' = similarity in appearance — light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke aren't compared for similarity; they're checked for a causal relation. 'Trace' = a small remaining amount, or a faint mark — 'a trace of X', not 'a trace between X and Y.'

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Test each option. 'No EVIDENCE has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke' — the preposition is wrong; evidence takes 'of' or 'for', not 'between.' 'No LINK has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke' — perfect fit; 'link between A and B' is the standard scientific phrase. 'No RESEMBLANCE has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke' — wrong concept; we're not comparing how the two LOOK alike. 'No TRACE has so far been found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke' — collocation breaks; 'trace of', not 'trace between.'

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Link between A and B' is the standard scientific collocation for a relation under investigation, and the sentence is reporting that no such relation was found between light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'evidence-based' from medical writing, 'evidence' feels like the natural noun for a research finding — and 'no evidence' is a common research phrase.
distractors.why_wrong: The preposition forces the issue: we say 'evidence FOR X' or 'evidence OF X', not 'evidence BETWEEN X AND Y.' The slot uses 'between', which collocates with 'link', 'connection', 'relationship'. Step 5 plugs and breaks the preposition.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the word 'between' is comparison — and 'resemblance between A and B' is grammatical English.
distractors.why_wrong: Resemblance is about similarity of appearance or nature. Light drinking and haemorrhagic stroke aren't compared for similarity — they're checked for whether one causes / correlates with the other. Wrong concept. Step 4's vocab gloss makes the distinction visible.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'No' + 'has so far been found' and reach for 'trace' because 'no trace' is a familiar phrase in detective and investigation contexts.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Trace' takes 'of' ('no trace of'), not 'between.' And researchers don't look for a 'trace' between two variables; they look for a relationship. Collocation and concept both fail. Step 5 plugs and breaks.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-035.technique: Cloze with 'No ___ has been found between A and B': the noun is almost always 'link', 'connection', 'association', or 'correlation'. The fixed preposition 'between' narrows the answer dramatically. Trigger: '___ between A and B' → relation-noun, not evidence-noun or trace-noun.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Students often pick by meaning alone and ignore the preposition. 'No evidence' is common — but 'no evidence BETWEEN' is not standard English. Always check that the gap-noun collocates with the surrounding preposition.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The 'Schools in Britain' introduction says grammar schools were 'once a center-piece of Britain's education system' but 'have largely been phased out over recent decades.' Largely phased out = not many left now. The answer is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What can be concluded about grammar schools from the introduction of the text?' Scope is locked to the introduction — the first short paragraph or two. So you're matching ONLY against that opening, not against the historical detail that comes later. Cut the search window early.

steps.title: Locate the introduction's claim
steps.text: The opening paragraph: 'British grammar schools are state-supported secondary schools that select their pupils through an exam taken at age 11, known as the 11-plus. Once a center-piece of Britain's education system, they have largely been phased out over recent decades.' Two beats: what they ARE (selective secondary schools), and what's HAPPENED TO THEM (largely phased out, but not entirely).

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Grammar schools were once central to British education. Over the last few decades they've mostly been shut down — 'largely' is the key word, meaning mostly but not all. So today there are still some around, but they're a small remnant of what they once were. Few left, not zero left.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Phased out' = gradually removed or discontinued over time. 'Largely' = mostly, but not entirely — leaves room for some to remain. 'Centre-piece' = the central, defining element of a larger system. So 'largely phased out' = the type still exists in small numbers, but its prominence is gone.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says their medieval roots STILL make them educational ideals — the intro mentions medieval roots later, but the intro PARAGRAPH itself doesn't make any ideal-status claim; it only says they were once central. B says 'there are not very many of them left these days' — direct gloss of 'largely been phased out over recent decades.' C says they were 'finally abolished in the mid-20th century' — wrong on two counts: not abolished (still some), and the timing is 'recent decades', not mid-20th century. D says their focus on languages is outmoded — the intro never mentions languages or any specific subject focus.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The intro's load-bearing claim is 'largely phased out over recent decades' — and 'not very many of them left these days' is the natural plain-English gloss.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'once a centre-piece' is to read it as 'still an ideal even though no longer dominant' — a kind of nostalgic survival.
distractors.why_wrong: The intro paragraph itself doesn't make an 'ideal' claim; it only registers former prominence and current decline. A imports a value judgment ('ideals') the intro doesn't state. Step 5 catches the import.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'phased out' as a process that ended, you might compress it into 'abolished' — and 'mid-20th century' might come from later mentions of the 1944 Education Act and 1965.
distractors.why_wrong: Two errors: 'largely phased out' explicitly leaves some still operating, and 'recent decades' (from 2010s vantage) is not the mid-20th century. The intro's two qualifiers — 'largely' and 'recent' — both block C. Step 4's vocab gloss anchors 'largely'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to associate 'grammar' with language teaching and conclude the schools' focus on languages has become outdated.
distractors.why_wrong: British 'grammar school' is a historical name, not a description of curriculum (they focused on academic studies generally, not languages specifically). And the intro paragraph doesn't discuss subject focus at all. Step 5 names the absent claim.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'introduction'-scoped items, match ONLY against the opening paragraph(s) — even if later passages add nuance. Trigger: 'in the introduction' or 'from the introduction' → ignore everything past the second paragraph.
host-2020-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Hedging words like 'largely', 'mostly', 'in part' do real work — they prevent the strong reading. 'Largely phased out' rules out both 'completely abolished' and 'still dominant'. Read hedges as gatekeepers against extreme options.

host-2020-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The 'Schools in Britain' passage explains that the 1944 Act 'set up three kinds of schools: grammar, technical and secondary moderns' — grammar for university-bound pupils, secondary moderns for those 'deemed suitable only for less skilled jobs.' The whole tripartite design SORTS pupils by perceived intellectual ability. That's exactly D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about the different types of secondary schools mentioned. So you're looking for a claim about the SYSTEM'S DESIGN — why three types existed and how pupils were sorted into them. The passage's middle paragraphs do this work.

steps.title: Locate the sorting logic
steps.text: Three sentences carry the structural claim. '[The 1944 Act]… set up three kinds of schools: grammar, technical and secondary moderns.' 'Children came to be divided between grammar schools, which focused on academic studies and whose pupils were destined for universities and more qualified professions, and secondary moderns, which were intended for children deemed suitable only for less skilled jobs.' 'Over time, it became clear that what separated pupils in the two types of schools was not ability, but class.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The 1944 system was built around the idea that pupils should be sorted into three tracks based on their intellectual level: academic (grammar), technical, and basic (secondary modern). Reality didn't match: the sorting ended up being driven by class, not ability. But the ORIGINAL INTENT was to match school types to different ability levels.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Three terms matter. 'Tripartite' (implied, not used) = three-part. 'Deemed suitable' = judged or considered fit for. 'Intellectual differences' (in option D) = differences in academic ability. So the system DESIGN deemed some pupils suited for academic study, others for technical training, others only for less skilled work — i.e., it sorted on perceived intellectual difference.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A says ability requirements were meant to be 'more or less identical' across the three school types — the OPPOSITE of the design; the whole point of having three types was different ability profiles. B says technical schools didn't differ substantially from grammar or secondary moderns — the passage doesn't make this comparison; it just notes that 'few technical schools were ever built.' C says technical schools were regarded as TOO DIFFICULT for most pupils — the passage doesn't claim this; it says few were built, not that they were intellectually elite. D says 'they were originally intended to select pupils on the basis of intellectual differences' — direct match for the tripartite design (academic-bound vs. less-skilled-bound).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The 1944 design split pupils into three tracks BASED ON ABILITY (in theory); class realities later disrupted that, but the original intent — selecting on intellectual differences — is exactly what D names.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'free education up to 15' and a unified Education Act, you might infer the Act tried to standardise ability requirements across school types.
distractors.why_wrong: Three school types with different academic profiles is the OPPOSITE of identical ability requirements. The grammar/technical/secondary-modern split is built on different ability assumptions. Step 3's paraphrase makes this explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'few technical schools were ever built' and infer that the three types collapsed into one in practice — so they were effectively the same.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Few built' is a quantity claim, not a similarity claim. The passage maintains a sharp design difference between grammar (academic, university-bound) and secondary moderns (less-skilled-bound). Step 5 catches the wrong inference.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to imagine technical schools as a difficult, niche option — 'technical' sounds like advanced specialisation that most students couldn't handle.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives no information about technical schools' difficulty level; it only mentions that few were built. C invents a difficulty claim. Step 5 names the absent evidence.
